# Transcrição: 11- Jack the Personal Assistant virtual

- **Fonte:** 11- Jack the Personal Assistant virtual
- **Duração:** 06:48
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

Hi, everybody. It's nice to see so many of my friends. I hope you're all doing well, and I hope you're safe. Thanks for being here tonight. I'm sitting at my desk. It's the very first day of school when Jack walks over to me. Jack is this 10-year-old, brown-haired mop of a boy. He looks down at me and he says, I'm going to be the greatest personal assistant you have ever had. And Jack has already ruined any chance of him ever even becoming the personal assistant because there's only enough room in this classroom for one arrogant person to this degree, and that person is me. I am the one who thinks highly of himself this year, not Jack in any way, so I'm not going to let Jack become my personal assistant at all. I haven't even announced that there is a job called personal assistant. I don't know where Jack has gotten the gumption to do this, but it's not going to happen. It's not going to happen because Jack could never be the greatest personal assistant of all time. That title goes to Michael McGinnis. About seven years ago, I made Michael McGinnis my personal assistant, and he was beyond compare the greatest of all time. I have a stage built into my classroom so that we can perform Shakespeare, and underneath that stage, the fifth grade teachers stuff all of our belongings throughout the year. And it's the personal assistant's job to go underneath the stage and pull things out when we need them because it's gross and it's dark and it's full of spiders. And so no one wants to go under there, so I make the personal assistant go under. So unlike day two of the school year, I tell Michael to go under and find something, and he pulls everything out from underneath the stage. And then he interviews all of the fifth grade teachers and asks them, what do you need this year? Like what are you going to most likely want? And he lines the edge of the stage with those items so they're easily reached. And then he creates a map of everything underneath the stage, so whenever I need it, he can get it quickly. This is something I should have thought about years ago, and he thought about it and did it for me. That is who Michael was. Michael was the kind of kid who a spelling test would be completed. I would put it on my desk and somehow he would take the spelling test, score it, and then put it back on my desk without me ever even seeing it happen. There was a day when we had indoor recess and I walked over to Michael and he was teaching a group of girls how to play poker. Texas hold them, a game that he knew I loved, but I could never teach because I would get fired for it. And so I watched him teach and when the principal walked in and said, why is there poker going on in your room? I said, I have nothing to do with it. It's the children's playtime. Michael is teaching them, please leave me alone. And it was beautiful. Like that was the kid Michael was. He was brilliant in every way. Jack is a nice kid, but he's not going to beat Michael. But unfortunately, I have to make Jack my personal assistant. He's unbelievable. There's an interview process and application and he goes out and gets personal reference letters. The first time this has ever happened, he gets letters from former teachers, from his parents, from his minister.

## [03:02 - 06:06]

I can't say no. So I agree, Jack, you can be my personal assistant, but there's just no way you're going to be the best personal assistant of all time. He tries like hell. I give him a lot of credit. The first thing he does is he takes over the phone because he knows I hate to answer the phone. So when the phone rings before I can even get to it, he picks it up and he says things like, hello, Mr. Dix's room. He is doing important work right now. Can I help you instead? Or hello, Mr. Dix's room. We're learning wonderful things. Why are you disturbing us? It was brilliant. I would tell Jack things like in the basement where students are not supposed to go. There is a box with a thing that I need for tomorrow's lesson. Go find it. And he just did it. He was really, genuinely an excellent personal assistant, not to Michael McGinnis' level, but really, really good. The problem with Jack was his enthusiasm began to irritate me because every day he would walk in and say, okay, Mr. Dix, what can I do next to make your day great? And there comes a point where that is too much, where it is a level of enthusiasm that makes you want to ring a person's neck. So by March, I wanted to ring Jack's neck. So there's a morning when he comes in smiling and says, so what can I do for you today, Mr. Dix? And I say, all right, Jack, sit down. I say, listen up. At my house, two towns away, there is a shed. And in that shed, there is a broken lawnmower that has been there for three years and I can't get rid of it. My wife refuses to allow me to illegally dump it on the side of the road. And my town dump will not take it because it has gas and oil and other things I don't understand. If you can get the mower out of my shed and make it disappear forever, you can be the greatest personal assistant of all time. And so I send him away with an impossible task, hoping he will never bother me again. The next day, Jack comes into the classroom, he sits down at my desk and he puts three pieces of paper in front of me. He touches the first one and he says, Mr. Dix, this person, if you call him, but I know you hate the phone, but if you call him, he will come and pick up the lawnmower for you and give you $50 for it. And I look closer at the paper and I can see what has happened. He's gone on Craigslist and he has found people that refurbish lawnmowers and pick them up at your home. He touches the second one and he goes, all you have to do for this guy is take that and he'll give you 20 bucks, but you have to be there when he gets there. And the third one he touches, he says, send this guy a text, he'll actually just take it out of your shed, you'll never even know he was there. I stare at these three pieces of paper, I stare at a kid who has just solved a problem that I have suffered with for three years, a problem I could not solve that he solved in less than 24 hours and he made me 50 bucks in the process. I tell Jack, Jack, you are the greatest personal assistant of all time. And I find myself thinking about Jack a lot these days. When I think about the world that we're given over to our kids and I think about everything,

## [06:08 - 06:48]

the things that we have done to this world, climate change and institutional racism, and now this terrible pandemic that has been handled by fools and idiots and incompetent human beings, I start to get sad and I lose hope sometimes. But when I lose hope, I think about Jack and all the Jacks there are in the world, young people, smarter and better than us, who I know will come along and fix this mess that we are handing over to them. I think about Jack a lot these days and he gives me hope for the future. Thank you. Thanks everybody. It's nice to see you all.
